does: This paper provides a conceptual framework for understanding how human annotations function in RLHF and preference-based alignment. The author distinguishes three normative models—extension (annotators implementing designers' values), evidence (annotators providing factual information about moral or social properties), and authority (annotators exercising independent power as population representatives)—and analyzes how each model should change annotation methodology including solicitation, validation, and aggregation practices. By surveying landmark RLHF papers, the work demonstrates how these models are implicitly embedded in existing systems and identifies failure modes arising from conflating distinct models without explicit justification.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in human-in-the-loop machine learning and recent literature on alignment methods that use human feedback, including foundational RLHF papers and constitutional AI approaches. It engages with philosophical and social science literature on representation, normativity, and legitimacy in decision-making systems. The paper responds to growing concerns about the role of human annotators in shaping large language models, extending earlier critiques of annotation practices in NLP that questioned whose values are represented and how disagreement is handled, while connecting to broader discussions in AI ethics about value aggregation and democratic input into AI system design.
enables: This conceptual framework provides a foundation for redesigning RLHF pipelines with explicit normative commitments. It opens pathways for developing annotation protocols tailored to each model—for instance, aggregation methods that differ depending on whether annotations represent individual value extensions versus collective authority. The work enables researchers to more carefully interrogate their implicit assumptions when designing alignment systems and points toward hybrid approaches that might combine multiple models appropriately. It also suggests future work on formal aggregation methods, methods for identifying which model is appropriate in different contexts, and ways to make these choices transparent to stakeholders affected by resulting AI systems.
